God can make strangers into friends; friends in times of distress. Those who are disliked because of simple manners are often more friendly than those with refined ways; and the behavior of non-Christians or people called barbarians condemns many in civilized nations who claim to be Christians. The people believed that Paul was a murderer, and that the viper was sent by Divine justice to punish him for his crime. They knew that there is a God who governs the world so that things do not happen by chance, no, not even the smallest event, but all by Divine direction; and that evil pursues sinners; that good works will be rewarded by God, and wicked works will be punished; also, that murder is a terrible crime which will not go unpunished for long. But they thought that all wicked people were punished in this life. Although some are made examples in this world to prove that there is a God and Providence, many are left unpunished to show that there is a future judgment. They also believed that everyone who suffered greatly in this life was a wicked person. Divine revelation clarifies this matter. Good men often suffer greatly in this life as a test and for the growth of their faith and patience. Observe Paul’s deliverance from danger. And thus, with the strength of Christ's grace, believers overcome Satan’s temptations with determination. When we disregard the criticism and insults of people and view them with contempt, having our consciences to reassure us, then, like Paul, we shake off the viper into the fire. It harms us not, unless it prevents us from fulfilling our duty. God made Paul remarkable among these people in this way, paving the way for the acceptance of the gospel. The Lord provides friends for his people wherever he leads them and makes them a blessing to those who are suffering.

The usual events of traveling are rarely worth telling; but the comfort of fellowship with other believers and kindness shown by friends deserve special mention. The Christians at Rome were not ashamed of Paul, nor afraid to acknowledge him because he was a prisoner; instead, they were even more careful to show him respect. He found great comfort in this. And if our friends are kind to us, God puts it into their hearts, and we should give Him the glory. When we see those even in unfamiliar places who bear Christ’s name, fear God, and serve Him, we should lift up our hearts in thanksgiving. How many powerful people have entered Rome, crowned and triumphant, who were actually a plague to the world! But here a good man enters Rome, chained as a poor captive, who was a greater blessing to the world than any mere human being. Is this not enough to make us lose interest in worldly favor? This may encourage God’s prisoners, so that He can give them favor in the eyes of those who hold them captive. When God does not quickly deliver his people from bondage, but makes it easy for them or allows them to endure it with ease, they have reason to be thankful.

It was for Paul's honor that those who examined his case acquitted him. In his appeal, he did not accuse his nation, but only sought to clear himself. True Christianity settles matters of common concern to all people and is not based on narrow opinions or private interests. It seeks no worldly benefit or advantage, but its gains are spiritual and eternal. It has always been the fate of Christ’s holy religion to be spoken against everywhere. Look through every town and village where Christ is proclaimed as the only Savior of mankind, and where people are called to follow Him in a new life, and you will see those who devote themselves to Christ still called a sect, a party, and reproached. This is the treatment they are sure to receive as long as there remains an ungodly person on earth.

Paul persuaded the Jews concerning Jesus. Some were influenced by his words, while others became hardened; some received the light, and others shut their eyes against it. The same has always been the effect of the gospel. Paul parted with them, noting that the Holy Spirit had accurately described their condition. Let all who hear the gospel and do not heed it tremble at their fate; for who will heal them if God does not? The Jews later engaged in much reasoning among themselves. Many reason, but they do not reason correctly. They criticize each other’s opinions, yet are unwilling to accept truth. Nor will people's reasoning convince them without the grace of God opening their understanding. While we mourn over such rejecters, we should rejoice that God’s salvation is sent to others who will receive it; and if we are among those recipients, we should be thankful to Him who has made us different. The apostle remained true to his principle: to know and preach only Christ and him crucified. Christians, when tempted from their main purpose, should ask themselves, ‘What does this have to do with the Lord Jesus?’ ‘How will it lead me to Him, and keep me walking in Him?’ The apostle preached himself not, but Christ, and he was not ashamed of the gospel of Christ. Although Paul was placed in a limited opportunity for usefulness, he was not hindered. Though a narrow door was opened to him, no one could shut it; and for many, it became an effective door, so that there were believers even in Nero’s household. We also learn from how God used Paul's imprisonment to advance the gospel. And not only the residents of Rome, but all the church of Christ, to the present day, and in every part of the world, have abundant reason to thank God that during the most mature period of his Christian life and experience, he was detained a prisoner. It was from his prison, probably chained hand and foot to the soldier who guarded him, that the apostle wrote the epistles to the Ephesians, Philippians, Colossians, and Hebrews; epistles showing, perhaps more than any others, the Christian love with which his heart overflowed, and the Christian experience with which his soul was filled. A believer today may have less triumph and less heavenly joy than the apostle did, but every follower of the same Savior is equally certain of safety and peace in the end. Let us seek to live more and more in the love of the Savior; let us strive to glorify Him by every action of our lives; and we shall surely, by His strength, be among those who now overcome our enemies; and by His free grace and mercy, be hereafter among the blessed company who will sit with Him upon his throne, even as He also has overcome, and is sitting on His Father’s throne at God's right hand forevermore.
